cit.q: inserting in the house [the leaves of] Neem wood along with sweet-fruited tree-turmeric

sense.note: For this word isolated in the early corpus the makes a separate entry, based on this passage, and a cross-reference to iruḷmaram [sic, without sandhi], “Ironwood of Ceylon (Mesua ferrea)”, without reference. Until actual occurrences of either word can be found, the likely explanation appears to be an etymological interpretation, reading iruḷ, “darkness” as equivalent to iravam, parallel form to the more frequent iravu or irā, “night”.

The gives yet another gloss for the same passage, iravan taḻai, "the foliage of iravam ", refraining from an identification of the tree, probably based on the gloss given in the first complete Puṟam commentary by Tuṛaicāmip Piḷḷai: iravamarattiṉ taḻai.

A more promising suggestion may be made, also on the basis of etymology, namely turmeric (Curcuma longa). The has a sub-entry under iravu with that meaning, evoking, as a possible explanation, Skt. niśā, “night”, also with a secondary meaning of “turmeric”. The combination of turmeric and neem is used until today in Ayurvedic medicine as an anti-infectant. If this is accepted, the would be the first place where these two plants are mentioned together.

Another puzzle which has to be solved in this context is the attribute tīm kaṉi, with “sweet fruits”. This definitely does not make sense with the meaning of ironwood. This could in theory refer to quite a number of trees, but in Caṅkam literature the most likely candidate is kāyā (Memecylon edule), not a tree known for its fruit but for its dark blue flower. Sweet fruit is not very likely in connection with turmeric either, but it would work likely with a plant related in name though not in family, the tree turmeric (Berberis aristata), which produces pretty red berries and can be used as an analgesic. Alternatively tīm kaṉi can be read in a figurative sense as referring to the good result to be expected from either iravam alone or it combination with vēmpu.
Note that neither the nor the add iravam under the words for mañcaḷ (turmeric).
